title: Norway and EU launch Arctic shipping corridor as Russia expands Northern Sea Route
author: Richard Milne
body:
OSLO — Norway, the European Union and a coalition of shipping companies unveiled a protected Arctic transport corridor on Thursday, responding to Moscow's efforts to commercialize the Northern Sea Route with icebreaker escorts and new ports near Murmansk.

Norwegian Prime Minister Jonas Gahr Store said the initiative would improve search-and-rescue coverage from Tromsø to Svalbard and require enhanced satellite monitoring by the European Space Agency. EU transport commissioner Apostolos Tzitzikostas said the bloc would fund ice-class pilot training and LNG bunkering stations in Hammerfest.

Russian state nuclear firm Rosatom, which operates the world's largest icebreaker fleet, said cargo traffic along the Northern Sea Route rose 18 percent last year, with shipments of oil, liquefied natural gas and coal destined for China and India. Gazprom Neft and Novatek highlighted exports from the Yamal Peninsula and the Arctic LNG 2 project, despite Western sanctions coordinated by the U.S. Treasury and UK Foreign Office.

U.S. Coast Guard commandant Linda Fagan, speaking in Reykjavik, warned that melting ice was creating new security gaps and called for joint patrols with Denmark, Canada and Iceland. Greenland's government said it supported environmental safeguards but opposed heavy militarization near Nuuk.

Environmental groups including Greenpeace and WWF criticized plans to expand drilling in the Barents Sea, arguing that a tanker accident near Varandey could devastate fisheries relied on by Indigenous Sami communities. Swedish climate activist Greta Thunberg joined a protest in Stockholm against insurers backing Arctic fossil fuel shipments.

Maersk and Hapag-Lloyd said they would trial low-emission vessels on feeder routes between Bergen and Rotterdam, while Japan's Mitsui O.S.K. Lines explored partnerships with South Korean yards for ammonia-ready tankers. China's COSCO already sent container ships through the Russian route to cut transit times to Rotterdam compared with the Suez Canal.

Analysts at the Arctic Institute said competition for Arctic shipping lanes could intensify rivalry between NATO members and Russia, particularly as China declared itself a "near-Arctic state" and invested in research stations in Norway's Finnmark region. Store urged dialogue through the Arctic Council, though cooperation with Russia remained suspended after the 2022 invasion of Ukraine.
